coredata.dc:title: Shell-core MoS2 nanosheets@Fe3O4 sphere heterostructure with exposed active edges for efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiao
Ding, Peng
Sun, Yanfang
Wang, Yansen
Wu, Yanhua
Guo, Jinxue
coredata.dc:description: Rational design and constructing advanced materials with intriguing nanostructures towards highly efficient and sustainable energy conversion and storage have received focused research efforts, but exploring effective strategies to accomplish this mission is still challenging. Aiming at this goal, we construct MoS2 nanosheets on Fe3O4 nanospheres via a SiO2 shell assisted sacrifice template method for the first time. The shell-core structured MoS2@Fe3O4 catalyst is well characterized by XRD, Raman, TEM, SEM, and XPS techniques. SEM and TEM show that MoS2 nanosheets possess edge exposed feature and defect-rich structure, assuring the maximum active sites for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Raman characterization reveals the chemical coupling between MoS2 and Fe3O4, indicating the formation of MoS2@Fe3O4 heterostructure at the shell-core interface. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) shows abundant defects at the heterostructures, which supplies more catalytic sites. The improved catalytic sites are proved by electrochemical capacitance measurements, and the enhanced catalytic activity over each site is also probed. Therefore, MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits remarkably enhanced catalytic activity for water splitting due to the above mentioned benefits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
Fe3O4
Hydrogen evolution
Shell-core structure
Heterostructure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of SiO2 template-assisted synthesis of shell-core structured MoS2@Fe3O4. (I) Coating Fe3O4 spheres with SiO2 shell via a facile Stöber method; (II) deposition of MoS2 nanosheets on Fe3O4 and simultaneously etching SiO2 with NH4F.
SEM images of (a) Fe3O4 spheres, (b) SiO2@Fe3O4, (c) MoS2@Fe3O4, and (d) pristine MoS2.
Raman spectra of MoS2@Fe3O4 and MoS2.
XPS spectra of (a) Mo 3d and S 2s and (b) S 2p in MoS2@Fe3O4.
(a) TEM image of MoS2@Fe3O4. The inset shows the top view, and the scale bar is 5 nm. (b) HRTEM images of MoS2@Fe3O4. (c) HRTEM image of defects on the edge plane of MoS2 nanosheet.

(a) Polarization curves of Fe3O4, MoS2, MoS2@Fe3O4, and Pt. (b) Tafel plots of MoS2 and MoS2@Fe3O4. (c) Polarization curves of the 1st and 1000th cycle obtained from MoS2@Fe3O4. (d) EIS spectra of MoS2, and MoS2@Fe3O4. The inset shows the corresponding equivalent circuit diagram.
Electrochemical capacitance measurements of (a) pristine MoS2, and (b) MoS2@Fe3O4. (c) Measured capacitive currents plotted as a function of scan rate. (d) TOF values of pristine MoS2 and MoS2@Fe3O4.
Electrochemical parameters of MoS2@Fe3O4 and the previous MoS2 catalysts.
The ECSA values of MoS2 and MoS2@Fe3O4 electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Shell-core MoS2 nanosheets@Fe3O4 sphere heterostructure with exposed active edges for efficient electrocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Rubber-plastics, Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubber-plastics, Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubber-plastics
Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Technology, Agricultural University of Hebei, Cangzhou 061100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design and constructing advanced materials with intriguing nanostructures towards highly efficient and sustainable energy conversion and storage have received focused research efforts, but exploring effective strategies to accomplish this mission is still challenging. Aiming at this goal, we construct MoS2 nanosheets on Fe3O4 nanospheres via a SiO2 shell assisted sacrifice template method for the first time. The shell-core structured MoS2@Fe3O4 catalyst is well characterized by XRD, Raman, TEM, SEM, and XPS techniques. SEM and TEM show that MoS2 nanosheets possess edge exposed feature and defect-rich structure, assuring the maximum active sites for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Raman characterization reveals the chemical coupling between MoS2 and Fe3O4, indicating the formation of MoS2@Fe3O4 heterostructure at the shell-core interface. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) shows abundant defects at the heterostructures, which supplies more catalytic sites. The improved catalytic sites are proved by electrochemical capacitance measurements, and the enhanced catalytic activity over each site is also probed. Therefore, MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits remarkably enhanced catalytic activity for water splitting due to the above mentioned benefits.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy sustainable production and storage are central to the sustainable development of human society. Molybdenum disulfide, possessing a typical two-dimensional (2D) layered structure of stacked S-Mo-S monolayer, has been considered as a promising candidate for applications in energy conversion and storage [1–3]. Due to the low onset potential and specific structural features, MoS2 based catalysts have received intensive research interests for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [4–26]. Previous reports have demonstrated that, the catalytic site numbers and electrical conductivity are key factors that determine the HER catalytic property. Carbon materials, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and et al., have been reasonably employed as conductive substrate to enhance the catalytic activity of MoS2 [5–12]. On the other hand, various MoS2 based catalysts with defect-rich structures or exposed edges have been developed to pursue improved catalytic active sites [13–20]. For instance, defect-rich MoS2 nanosheets with robust HER activity have been prepared via a solvothermal method [15]. Xie and co-workers have developed vertically aligned MoS2 nanowalls with exposed edge sites to achieve improved HER catalytic activity [20]. Recently, both computational and experimental studies have demonstrated that, doping transition metals (Fe, Co, Ni et al.) into MoS2 or constructing the hybrids of MoS2 with other metal sulfides could tune its electronic and structural features, thus boosting the HER catalytic performance [21–26]. Unfortunately, the studies on these kinds of transition metal doped MoS2 catalysts are limited by the rational structure design and proper component selection.

Constructing shell-core structured materials has been believed as an effective approach to achieve improved electrochemical properties, because of the structural advantages and the potential synergistic effect between different components [27]. Recently, some carbonous materials, such as N-doped carbon nanoboxes, carbon nanotubes, and polyaniline nanowires, have been used to support MoS2 for the fabrication of shell-core structures and applications for electrochemical water splitting [7,28,29]. Besides, the shell-core structured MoS2|NiS|MoO3 nanowires with high intrinsic activity have also been synthesized as good electrocatalysts [30]. Therefore, selection of proper materials with motivating effect (e.g. oxides/sulfides of Fe, Co, Ni) to support MoS2 nanosheets as shell-core structured catalysts could reasonably obtain both synergistic effect and structural advantages of highly exposed active centers and large surface area for HER. However, the design and construction of such MoS2 based shell-core structures have rarely been reported and are desirable to be explored.

In the present work, we rationally design and develop the SiO2 shell assisted sacrifice template method to construct MoS2 nanosheets@Fe3O4 nanosphere shell-core nanostructures as advanced materials for electrochemical HER. The Fe3O4 sphere serves as the support for anchor of edge exposed MoS2 nanosheets and enhances the charge transfer. The SiO2 sacrifice template synthesis strategy assures the well anchor of MoS2 nanosheets on the surface of Fe3O4 nanospheres. The successful formation of heterostructure between MoS2 and Fe3O4 induces defect-rich feature and rapid charge exchange. Therefore, enhanced HER catalytic activity is reasonably obtained from the present hybrid catalyst.

The shell-core structured MoS2@Fe3O4 is prepared using SiO2 shell as sacrifice template, which is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, FeCl3·6H2O (3.3 g), sodium citrate (0.6 g), and anhydrous sodium acetate (6 g) are dissolved into 60 mL of ethylene glycol with vigorous stirring, which is then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) for heating at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooled to room temperature, the resultant black precipitates of Fe3O4 are harvested by centrifugation, washed with water and ethanol, and vacuum dried at 80 °C. Secondly, the obtained Fe3O4 nanosphers (0.2 g) are dispersed into a mixed solution containing 80 mL of ethanol and 16 mL of deionized water. After 4 mL of ammonia solution (30%) is added, 2 mL of tetraethylorthosilicate is then added in 15 min under ultrasonic. After 3 h, the products of shell-core structured SiO2@Fe3O4 are harvested by magnet and vacuum dried. Finally, SiO2@Fe3O4 (0.1 g), Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.16 g), and thioacetamide (0.15 g) are dissolved in 60 mL of water. After ultrasonic-treatment of 1 h, the suspension is added with NH4F (0.022 g) and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) for heating at 200 °C for 12 h. The precipitates of MoS2@Fe3O4 are harvested, washed, and vacuum dried at 80 °C. And the SiO2 sacrifice template is etched by HF that is generated from NH4F during the hydrothermal process. Pristine MoS2 is prepared via a similar method using Na2MoO4·2H2O and thioacetamide for comparison.

The crystallographic information of MoS2@Fe3O4 is collected from X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Philips X’-pert X-ray diffractometer). Raman spectroscopy is performed on a confocal microprobe Raman system (LabRam-e010, 632 nm as the excitation source). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) is obtained on a JEOL JSM-7500F. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) is conducted on a JEOL, JEM-2100. The composition contents of MoS2@Fe3O4 composite are determined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES, Perkin-Elmer Optima 3100 XL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is performed on a RBD upgrade PHI-5000c ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Mg Kα radiation (hν = 1253.6 eV).

The electrochemical HER experiments are conducted on CHI760D (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) with a three-electrode cell. The MoS2@Fe3O4 (4 mg) is dispersed into a mixed solution containing 50 μL Nafion solution (5 wt%) and 1.25 mL water under ultrasonic to obtain the catalyst ink. A portion of 5 μL ink (containing 20 μg of catalyst) is drop-casted on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter), which is used as the working electrode after dried at room temperature. The counter electrode and reference electrode are a graphite rod and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE), respectively. Linear sweep voltammetry are tested in 0.50 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 5.00 mV s−1 (purged with pure N2). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is obtained at the frequency ranging from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz in 0.5 M H2SO4 at the overpotential of 300 mV. All the potentials are calibrated to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) to assess the HER performance of catalysts.

The electrochemical capacitance measurements are performed over the potential between 0.025 and 0.325 V versus RHE at varied scan rates of 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, and 140 mV s−1. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) is calculated based on the obtained specific capacitance [31]. And the per site turnover frequency (TOF) is also determined by the specific current density [31].

Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic synthesis of MoS2@Fe3O4. The SiO2 shell sacrifice template method is developed to construct the designed shell-core MoS2@Fe3O4. In the present synthesis strategy, SiO2 shell plays an important role for the controlled deposition of MoS2 nanosheets on the surface of Fe3O4 spheres, assuring the specific shell-core architecture as well as good coupling between them.

The morphologies of products at different synthesis steps are characterized with SEM. In Fig. 2 a, monodisperse Fe3O4 spheres show narrow size distribution, with diameters of 250–300 nm. After coated with SiO2 shell (Fig. 2b), shell-core structured SiO2@Fe3O4 spheres show significantly smoother surface than Fe3O4, indicating the successful coating of SiO2 shell on Fe3O4 spheres. In comparison with pristine Fe3O4, SiO2@Fe3O4 shows negligible changes in diameter, suggesting the thin layer of SiO2 shell. For the final products (Fig. 2c), MoS2@Fe3O4 spheres with rough surface are observed. This should be assigned to the coated MoS2 nanosheets shell. Moreover, the diameters of these spheres increase to about 300–350 nm, further confirming the formation of MoS2@Fe3O4. In the SEM image of pristine MoS2 (Fig. 2d), particles agglomerated by nanosheets are observed. In comparison with MoS2@Fe3O4, the pristine MoS2 particles certainly possess much smaller surface area and lower catalytic sites.

In the XRD pattern of MoS2@Fe3O4 (Fig. 3 ), all the diffraction peaks can be divided into two phases, which are well indexed to 2-H MoS2 (PDF 37-1492) or cubic Fe3O4 (PDF 65-3107), respectively. No other peak for impurity phase can be observed. The Raman spectra of MoS2@Fe3O4 and MoS2 are obtained and shown in Fig. 4 . For pristine MoS2, the characteristic peaks of MoS2 can be observed at around 370 cm−1 and 421 cm−1, relating to the E1 2g and A1g vibrational modes, respectively [32]. In the Raman spectroscopy of hybrid catalyst, the peak corresponding to the A1g vibrational mode of Fe3O4 is detected at 670 cm−1, in addition the characteristic peaks of MoS2 [33,34]. Interestingly, some Raman peaks located at 342, 785, 824, 930, and 981 cm−1 are also observed, which cannot be indexed to MoS2 or Fe3O4. This should be ascribed to the formation of Mo-O, Mo-O2, and Mo-O3 bonds [10]. The Raman results suggest the good coupling and the formation of heterostructure between MoS2 and Fe3O4, which will arouse the synergistic effect between them and boost the catalytic activity of per active sites for MoS2, thus inducing improved HER catalytic activity. The XPS measurements are conducted to reveal the valence state of the hybrid catalyst, and the mole ratio of Mo:S is also determined to be about 1:2. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of Fig. 5 a, the peak located at ∼226.5 eV corresponds to the S 2s. Two peaks corresponding to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 components of Mo4+ are detected at 229.3 and 232.4 eV, respectively [11]. Moreover, a peak at about 236.4 eV is observed, which is assigned to the Mo6+ species, indicating the partial oxidation of Mo4+ [11]. In the S 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 5b), two peaks at 162.1 and 163.2 eV are identified as S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2−, respectively. Interestingly, a weak peak related to the S4+ is observed at 168.8 eV, due to the partial oxidation of S2−, which is consistent with the Mo 3d result.

The microstructure of MoS2@Fe3O4 is further observed with TEM. In the TEM image of Fig. 6 a, MoS2@Fe3O4 displays specific shell-core structure with spherical morphology. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 6b) shows the magnified part labeled with red rectangle in Fig. 6a. Clearly, ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets with ripples morphology are vertically grown on the surface of Fe3O4, with a typical interlayer distance of 0.62 nm. This edge terminated structure assures the maximally exposed edges, which is beneficial for maximizing the active sites for catalytic reaction. The MoS2 shell possesses a thickness ranging from about 10 to 20 nm. As referred in Fig. 6b, the highly exposed lattice of Fe3O4 is determined to be (111) crystal plane with a d spacing of 0.48 nm. Moreover, the inner edge of MoS2 nanosheets is terminated on the surface of Fe3O4, forming a heterostructure between them. This is in accordance with the Raman result. Importantly, one can see from Fig. 6c that abundant defects (labeled by white arrows) exist on the edge plane of MoS2 nanosheets, which induce more active sites for HER. Moreover, the region marked between two red lines clearly shows the heterostructure of MoS2 and Fe3O4, in consistent with the Raman result.

To highlight the structural advantages and heterostructure benefit, the electrocatalytic HER measurements of the present shell-core structured catalyst are conducted. The polarization curve of MoS2@Fe3O4 is displayed in Fig. 7 a, and samples of pristine MoS2 and Fe3O4 are also tested for comparison. Significantly, MoS2@Fe3O4 delivers the much lower onset potential of 110 mV than that of 200 mV for MoS2, whereas Fe3O4 shows negligible catalytic activity. The HER catalytic current densities of the three samples show the similar order. For instance, MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits a high current density of 48.4 mA cm−2 at a potential of −300 mV, which is much higher than that of MoS2 (9.6 mA cm−2) and Fe3O4 (1.1 mA cm−2). The remarkable promotional effects on HER catalytic activity should be assigned to the synergistic effect between the MoS2@Fe3O4 heterostructure. The Fe3O4 spherical substrate not only ensures the exposed edge sites of MoS2 nanosheets, but also improves the numbers of active site. Moreover, the Fe atoms in the heterostructure could reduce the Gibbs free energy of adsorbed atomic hydrogen on the S-edge of MoS2, thus inducing improved HER catalytic activity [21]. The catalytic current densities of state-of-the-art MoS2 based catalysts and the present MoS2@Fe3O4 are summarized in Table 1 . Clearly, the MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits the lowest onset overpotential, due to the superior structure feature and synergistic effect of the heterostructure. The catalytic current density of MoS2@Fe3O4 is also among the best results for MoS2 based catalyst [10,19,22]. Noting that, the content of MoS2 in the composite catalyst is only ∼9%, which is determined by ICP-AES. The true catalytic current density can be calculated to be an amazing high value of 538 mA cm−2 based on this. The excellent catalytic activity of MoS2@Fe3O4 should be attributed to its structural advantages, such as the exposed edges and defect-rich features that supply improved active sites for HER, as well as the heterostructure between the shell-core components that induce the synergistic effect for enhanced catalytic activity of per catalytic units.

The Tafel plots of MoS2@Fe3O4 and MoS2 are used here to detect the rate-limiting step of the electrocatalytic HER process. Electrochemical HER in acidic electrolytes is generally believed to go through three possible reaction steps [10], including the first discharge step of Volmer reaction (H3O+ +e− → Hads + H2O) with a Tafel slope of 120 mV dec−1, the following reaction of Heyrovsky reaction (Hads + H3O+ +e− → H2 + H2O) with 40 mV decade−1, or Tafel reaction (Hads + Hads → H2) with 30 mV decade−1. One can see from Fig. 7b, MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits a Tafel slope of 52 mV dec−1. According to the previous reports [10], the HER reaction on MoS2@Fe3O4 should probably go through the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism. In comparison with the value of 94 mV dec−1 for pristine MoS2, the much decreased Tafel slope of MoS2@Fe3O4 means enhanced reaction kinetic and improved HER catalytic activity, which is derived from the designed structural advantages.

The HER durability, as a crucial criterion, is also performed to evaluate the electrocatalyst property. Fig. 7c displays the polarization curves at the initial and after 1000 cycles for MoS2@Fe3O4. The two curves are almost overlapped, indicating that no catalytic activity fade after the long-term test of 1000 cycles. The excellent catalytic activity and durability of our present MoS2@Fe3O4 make it a promising candidate for noble-metal-free and high-performance HER catalyst. Moreover, the EIS spectra (Fig. 7d) are obtained to reveal the electrode kinetics of HER. The corresponding equivalent circuit diagram is shown in the inset of Fig. 7d. The charge-transfer impedance (RCT) can reflect the kinetics of electrochemical HER on different electrodes, and a lower RCT value indicates the faster reaction rate. Therefore, MoS2@Fe3O4 electrode with lower RCT of 357 Ω possesses faster HER kinetics than MoS2 electrode with RCT of 1636 Ω, in consistent with their catalytic performance. This may be assigned to the better conductivity of Fe3O4 than MoS2, as well as the rapid charge exchange between the MoS2@Fe3O4 heterostructure.

To better highlight the improvement of edge-exposed structures on catalytic sites, the electrochemical capacitance measurements of MoS2@Fe3O4 and pristine MoS2 are performed to quantitatively determine their electrochemically active surface area. Fig. 8 b shows the non-faradic capacitive current associated with the electrochemical double layer charging of MoS2@Fe3O4, which is much larger than that of MoS2 (Fig. 8a). In Fig. 8c, the obtained capacitive currents of MoS2 and MoS2@Fe3O4 catalysts are plotted as a function of scan rate. Clearly, the dependence of current plots on the scan rate is linear for both MoS2@Fe3O4 and MoS2, in accordance with capacitive charging behavior. As shown in Table 2 , MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits a larger double layer capacitance of 12700 μF cm−2, which is 16.5 fold as high as that (770 μF cm−2) for MoS2, indicating the amazing increased numbers of catalytic sites. The remarkably increased active sites of MoS2@Fe3O4 can be assigned to two contributions, including the main contribution that is derived from the exposed edges and the additional section of defects.

In addition to the numbers of active sites, the quality of per active sites is another crucial factor that determines the activity of HER catalysts. Therefore, the TOF values of MoS2@Fe3O4 catalyst is measured, and the values of bare MoS2 is also presented for comparison. One can see from Fig. 8d that, MoS2@Fe3O4 exhibits much higher TOF values than pristine MoS2 at high potential region (after 120 mV), indicating that MoS2@Fe3O4 possesses higher intrinsic HER activity for per catalytic site [31]. This should be attributed to the synergistic effect derived from the MoS2@Fe3O4 heterostructure. Therefore, the rational structural design endows the shell-core 2D MoS2 nanosheets coated Fe3O4 nanosphers with combined benefits of improved active sites and enhanced catalytic activity for per active site, thus assuring robust electrochemical HER activity.

In summary, shell-core structured MoS2 nanosheets@Fe3O4 nanospheres have been explored using the SiO2 shell assisted sacrifice template method for the first time, which highlights the promoting effects of nanostructure engineering and heterostructure on improving the electrocatalytic activity of MoS2 based catalysts. The designed edge-exposed structure with defect-rich feature can maximally expose the catalytic sites, which is confirmed by both TEM characterization and the electrochemical capacitance measurements. Interestingly, the unexpected formation of heterostructure endows synergistic effect for enhanced catalytic activity for per active site, which is revealed by the TOF calculation. Based on these combined advantages, MoS2@Fe3O4 demonstrates remarkably improved electrocatalytic performance, making it a promising candidate for noble-metal-free and high-performance HER catalyst. In addition, the developed synthesis strategy could be used for the construction of other shell-core structured metal sulfides@metal oxides nanostructures with heterostructures for potential applications in catalysis, lithium ion batteries, and sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for the financial support from Natural Science Foundation (ZR2014JL015, 2016GGX104019, ZR2014EMM004) of Shandong Province.
